planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade after frost; elephant ear begonia requires good air circulation to prevent fungal issues — in humid climates, overhead watering should be avoided and plants should be spaced to allow air movement around the hairy leaves, which can trap moisture if too crowded.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade; elephant ear begonia (Begonia scharffii, syn. B. haageana) is one of the largest and most architecturally impressive of the shrub begonias — it grows with a stout, upright, branching, cane-like habit reaching 3–5 feet, with enormous, asymmetric, heart-shaped leaves that can reach 12 inches or more in length, rich olive-green on the upper surface and vivid deep-red on the undersides, with both surfaces covered with soft, silvery-white hairs that give the foliage a luminous, textured quality in low light. Large, pendant clusters of white to pale-pink flowers with prominent yellow stamens are produced at branch tips throughout the warm season. The hairy, large-leaved, red-backed foliage and the bold shrubby habit combine to make it a commanding specimen in shaded gardens, large containers, and conservatories. It tolerates lower humidity better than most shade-growing tropical plants and is one of the easier large begonias to grow as an overwintering indoor houseplant in cool climates. An outstanding architectural shade specimen for zone 10–12 outdoor gardens.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or water, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade after frost; fuchsia begonia is more drought-tolerant than most begonias and performs better in lower-humidity environments — allow the top inch of soil to dry slightly between waterings to prevent root rot in the fibrous root system.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade; fuchsia begonia is a Venezuelan shrubby begonia uniquely suited to milder and drier conditions than most tropical begonias — it produces a graceful, freely branching, shrubby habit reaching 2–4 feet with small, oval, glossy, bright-green, slightly toothed leaves and an abundance of pendant, fuchsia-like, coral-red to bright-pink flower clusters hanging on long, slender pedicels throughout the warm season and nearly year-round in frost-free climates. The delicate, drooping flower clusters have a jewel-like quality that closely resembles fuchsia flowers in their pendant form and rich coloring — hence the common name — but this plant is better adapted to warmth and partial shade than true fuchsias. It is the most drought-tolerant of the commonly grown shrub begonias, making it better suited to indoor conditions and gardens with inconsistent irrigation. An outstanding specimen in hanging baskets, tall containers, and sheltered outdoor shade gardens in zone 10–12.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring or plant container specimens in wet to moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil or submerged in a water feature; water canna tolerates standing water up to 6 inches deep and can be grown in containers submerged in garden ponds — it is one of the few cannas that actually thrives in permanently flooded conditions and can be treated as an aquatic plant in shallow ponds and water features.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in wet to moist, humus-rich, fertile soil or in shallow water in full sun to partial shade; water canna (Louisiana canna) is a wetland and aquatic canna native to South America and the Caribbean, growing naturally at the edges of streams and ponds and in shallow standing water — it differs from garden hybrid cannas in its distinctively narrow, lance-shaped, blue-green to gray-green, powdery-glaucous foliage (the species name 'glauca' refers to this gray-blue waxy leaf bloom) and its slender, elegant, pale-to-deep yellow, narrow-petaled flowers that are considerably more graceful and refined than the large-flowered hybrid cannas. Unlike garden hybrid cannas, water canna tolerates permanently boggy, saturated, or even inundated soils and can be grown in containers standing in several inches of water in garden ponds and water features. It is also more shade-tolerant than hybrid cannas, performing creditably in partial shade. It is one parent of the heat-tolerant Louisiana hybrid aquatic cannas, which inherit its moisture-tolerance. Hardy to approximately 10°F in zone 7 with roots protected by water or mulch.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide corms or separate offsets in spring, or plant container-grown specimens in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade after frost; dwarf velvet taro is considerably more shade-tolerant than most colocasias and performs at its ornamental best in partial to full shade — full sun bleaches the dark, velvety leaf surface and reduces the contrast of the dark-purple vein pattern.

directions: Divide corms or plant container specimens in moist to wet, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial to full shade; dwarf velvet taro is a smaller, more refined, and considerably more shade-tolerant Colocasia species from the Himalayan foothills and Northeast India, growing as a compact, 18–30-inch clump with distinctly smaller and more refined leaves than the large taro (C. esculenta). The leaves are broadly oval, deep, velvety-green in color with a distinctive matte texture and prominent dark-purple to near-black veining that traces an elaborate, ornamental pattern against the intensely dark-green surface — creating a sophisticated, jewel-like foliage effect markedly different from the plain green of common taro. The cultivar 'Jenningsii' is particularly noted for its dark, nearly black veining pattern. Unlike the large-scale giant colocasias, this species grows at a garden scale well-suited to smaller shade borders and container culture. It spreads by stolons into moderate, well-behaved clumps and is more cold-tolerant than many ornamental aroids, surviving to approximately 20°F in zone 8 with rhizomes mulched.

planting.note: Plant tubers 1–2 inches deep in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade after frost; voodoo taro emerges rapidly once soil temperatures warm above 65°F — if tubers are planted when the soil is still cool, they will rot rather than sprout. The inflorescence, which emerges before or simultaneously with the leaves, has a pungent, unpleasant odor that is brief and fades once pollination is complete.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; voodoo taro (red stem taro) is a tropical aroid native to South and Southeast Asia with an unusual combination of ornamental foliage and jack-in-the-pulpit-style flowers — the deeply three-lobed, triangular, arrow-shaped, rich dark-green leaves are held on erect, slender, attractively mottled, dark-green and purple petioles and are prominently veined, creating a bold, tropical texture in the shade garden. Before or alongside the foliage, the plant produces a distinctive inflorescence: a narrow, dark-maroon to green spathe surrounding a long, filiform, deep-purple, tail-like appendage at the tip of the spadix, with a brief but pungent, carrion-like scent that attracts fly pollinators (the characteristic pollination strategy of many Araceae). After pollination, clusters of bright-red berries form on the lower portion of the spadix and remain ornamental for several weeks. The plant goes dormant in winter and reemerges in spring when soils warm. An unusual and interesting shade-garden aroid for collectors and those who appreciate the quirky botanical spectacle of carrion-scented, fly-pollinated tropical plants in zone 9–12.
